one_line_takeaway: Reframing a visual reward model as a VLM's P('yes') next-token probability lets it scale to 26B parameters and richer context, yielding monotonic generation-quality gains and reduced RLHF mode collapse across image and video tasks.
key_finding: Scaling the reward model along both size and context axes produces monotonic, consistent gains in final generation quality AND increased resistance to reward hacking. Larger RMs sustain higher reward variance late in RL training (a proxy for continued policy exploration), which the authors interpret as avoiding the mode collapse that smaller RMs fall into. The generative paradigm beats the regression paradigm at equal scale, and OOD RM accuracy is a better predictor of downstream RL gains than ID accuracy (ID accuracy was non-monotonic across 1B/2B/4B).
core_contribution: RewardDance is a scalable reward-modeling (RM) framework for visual generation (text-to-image, text-to-video, image-to-video). Its central idea is a generative reward paradigm: instead of appending a regression head to a Vision-Language Model (VLM) and training with a Bradley-Terry loss, the reward score is reformulated as the VLM's predicted probability of a 'yes' token, r = P('yes' | image1, image2, prompt, instruction), where 'yes' means the generated image beats a reference image under a stated criterion. This natively aligns the reward objective with the VLM's autoregressive next-token-prediction mechanism, which the authors argue removes the architectural and paradigm-mismatch bottlenecks of prior CLIP-based and regression-head VLM RMs. The alignment unlocks scaling on two axes: (1) model scaling of the RM from 1B to 26B parameters (InternVL backbones), and (2) context scaling via task-aware instructions, reference examples, and chain-of-thought (CoT) reasoning. Per the paper's Table 1, RewardDance is claimed to be the first visual-generation framework to combine a generative paradigm with comprehensive scalability across both RM size and reward-context dimensions.
threat_model: None. This is not a security/adversarial paper and it defines no attacker, asset, or trust boundary. The term 'reward hacking' here denotes an internal RLHF optimization pathology (the policy diffusion model exploiting loopholes in a static reward signal to inflate scores without true quality gains, leading to mode collapse), not an adversary manipulating a deployed system. No security frame should be imposed.
limitations: Max RM size is 26B; the authors themselves flag 70B/100B as untested future work. The 'resistance to reward hacking' claim rests on an indirect proxy (late-training reward variance / error-bar width) rather than a direct measurement of hacked vs. genuine quality, and the causal interpretation of variance as healthy exploration is asserted rather than isolated. Almost all headline metrics are human-preference scores on the authors' own Bench-240 and SeedVideoBench-1.0, and the strongest results come on ByteDance's own proprietary Seedream/Seedance base models, so external reproducibility is limited. Work is confined to the alignment capability dimension (not motion or aesthetics). RM ID accuracy did not scale monotonically, which the paper acknowledges but does not fully explain.

datasets_used: Bench-240 (240 text-to-image prompts, from Seedream 2.0/3.0; max score 100)
SeedVideoBench-1.0 (300 prompts for both T2V and I2V, from Seedance 1.0)
In-Domain (ID) preference set: 2,500 held-out sample pairs from RewardDance Stage-2 training data
Out-Of-Domain (OOD) preference set: >4,000 pairs sampled from ImageReward and HPS public benchmarks
CoT reasoning training data distilled from teacher model SEED-VL 1.5
quantitative_results: RL fine-tuning alignment (FLUX.1-dev): 67.0 (no RM) to 73.6 with 26B RM (+6.6)
RL fine-tuning alignment (Seedream-3.0-SFT): 74.1 to 84.8 with 26B RM (+10.7)
Test-time scaling alignment (Seedream-3.0-SFT): 74.1 to 80.5 with 26B RM (+6.4)
RM OOD accuracy: 69.10% (1B) rising to 80.90% (26B); RM ID accuracy 64.70% (1B) to 78.44% (26B), non-monotonic across mid sizes
Video GSB improvement over Seedance-1.0-SFT: T2V +28% (1B) to +49% (26B); I2V +29% (1B) to +47% (26B)
GenEval overall: Seedream-3.0 w/ RewardDance 0.79 (+0.10), FLUX.1-dev w/ RewardDance 0.75 (+0.09), vs SD-v3 0.74
Bench-240 overall: Seedream-3.0 w/ RewardDance 0.848, vs Imagen 3 0.79, Luma 0.77, Midjourney V6.1 0.63
SeedVideoBench Video-Text Alignment: Seedance-1.0 w/ RewardDance T2V 1.66 (vs Veo-3.0 1.63, Kling 2.1 1.57); I2V 1.65 (tie with Kling 2.1)
Ablation - paradigm shift (8B RM): regressive to generative FLUX.1-dev 70.8 to 71.6, Seedream 80.7 to 81.0; adding reference images FLUX 71.6 to 73.0, Seedream 81.0 to 81.6
Ablation - CoT finetuning (Seedream-3.0): 81.6 to 83.6; Best-of-16 Top-2 reference reaches 83.6
DiT-size interaction: scaling RM 8B to 26B gives Seedream-3.0 +3.92% (81.6 to 84.8) vs Seedream-3.0-Lite +2.75% (65.5 to 67.3)
Late-training reward variance grows with RM size (e.g., generative 26B std ~5.8e-2 vs regressive 2B ~7.2e-3), used as the reward-hacking-resistance indicator
baselines_compared: RM baselines (Table 1): ImageReward, PickScore, HPSv2 (CLIP regressive); VisionReward, VideoAlign, HPSv3, WorldPM (VLM regressive); DeepSeek-GRM, Pairwise RM, UnifiedReward (generative)
Internal RM ablation baselines: pointwise regressive vs pointwise generative vs pairwise generative; ReFL alignment; Best-of-N reference variants
Image generation SOTA: LlamaGen, SD-v1.5/v2.1/v3, PixArt-alpha, DALL-E 2/3, Emu3-Gen, SDXL, FLUX.1-dev/FLUX1.1, Imagen 3, Luma, Ideogram, Midjourney V6.1, Recraft, Hunyuan, Seedream 2.0, Qwen-Image
Video generation SOTA: Kling 2.1/2.0 Master, Veo-3.0/2.0, Wan 2.1, Sora, RunwayGen 4
